planting.note: Direct sow in loose, stone-free soil; keep the surface moist until seedlings emerge.

directions: Sow seed shallowly directly in the garden and thin seedlings to about 2 inches apart once they reach a couple inches tall. This sweet, nearly coreless heirloom develops best in deep, loose, well-drained soil free of rocks that can fork the roots. Keep the seedbed evenly moist during the slow germination period, which can take up to three weeks. Harvest when roots reach about six to seven inches and full color for the tenderest, sweetest flavor. Successive sowings from spring through midsummer extend the harvest.

planting.note: Start indoors early; transplant only after nights stay reliably warm.

directions: Start seed indoors eight or more weeks before the last frost, as this Caribbean chile needs warm soil and a long season to ripen. Transplant into the warmest part of the garden after the soil and nights have settled into the 60s. The fiery, fruity fruits mature from green to bright yellow-orange or red, so wait for full color for maximum heat and flavor. Consistent watering and a steady feed produce the heaviest yields. Wear gloves when handling the exceptionally hot pods.

planting.note: Start indoors and set out after all danger of frost has passed.

directions: Start seed indoors about six to eight weeks ahead of transplanting and provide bright light and warmth for sturdy seedlings. Move plants outdoors once the soil warms and frost danger has passed, spacing them about a foot and a half apart. These prolific plants set many slender, spicy peppers used green for salsas and pico de gallo. Pick fruits green for the classic sharp heat, or leave them to ripen red for a slightly sweeter kick. Steady moisture and full sun keep production high through the season.

planting.note: Direct sow after frost when soil is warm; provide a tall trellis.

directions: Direct sow seed after the last frost once the soil is warm, planting alongside a sturdy trellis or pole seven to nine feet tall. This All-America Selections winner bears clusters of tender four-to-six-inch pods well ahead of many standard pole beans. Pick pods young and often to keep the vines producing over a long season. Even, moderate watering during flowering and pod set gives the best yields. Avoid handling the plants when wet to reduce the spread of disease.

planting.note: Plant cloves in fall a few weeks before the ground freezes.

directions: Break bulbs into individual cloves and plant them pointed end up about two inches deep in fall, a few weeks before the ground freezes. This early-maturing Turban-type hardneck yields white bulbs with six to eight cloves and matures noticeably faster than most hardnecks. Mulch after planting to protect the cloves through winter and conserve moisture. Cut the curling scapes in early summer to direct energy into bulb sizing. Harvest when the lower leaves brown, then cure the bulbs in a dry, airy place.

planting.note: Heat-tolerant, so it can be sown into late spring and summer.

directions: Sow seed shallowly in loose, deep soil and keep the surface moist during the slow germination. Unlike many carrots, this heat-tolerant Kuroda type can be sown well into late spring and summer for fall harvests. Thin seedlings to about two inches so the tapered five-inch roots with broad shoulders can size up. Steady moisture prevents cracking and keeps the roots sweet and tender. Harvest before roots become oversized for the best texture.

planting.note: Surface sow in early spring or fall; seed needs light to germinate.

directions: Scatter the tiny seed on the soil surface in early spring or fall, pressing lightly but not covering since it needs light to sprout. This 1880s heirloom bears delicate two-to-three-inch blossoms on slender, nodding stems that dance in the breeze. Poppies resent transplanting, so always direct sow where they are to grow and thin gently. As a carefree annual it readily self-sows, making it ideal for naturalized areas and meadow plantings. Water sparingly once established, as these plants tolerate lean, dry conditions.

planting.note: Start indoors and transplant after frost danger passes.

directions: Start seed indoors six to eight weeks before the last frost and transplant seedlings out after the soil warms. This popular Japanese frying pepper produces heavy sets of slender two-to-four-inch fruits with a mild, citrusy, slightly smoky flavor. Harvest peppers green and small for the best flavor when blistered in a hot pan or on the grill. Roughly one in ten pods surprises with mild heat, adding to the fun. Pick frequently to keep the highly productive plants setting new fruit.

planting.note: Fall-plant cloves; softnecks also do well in milder winter regions.

directions: Separate bulbs into cloves and plant them pointed end up about two inches deep in fall. This Artichoke-type softneck forms twelve to twenty cloves per bulb with classic pearl-white papery skin sometimes streaked purple. Softnecks store especially well and are ideal for braiding, and they perform reliably in milder-winter regions. Mulch for winter protection and keep the bed weed-free and evenly moist through spring. Harvest when the lower leaves yellow, then cure in a dry, ventilated spot before storing.

planting.note: Fall-plant cloves; adaptable to cold, hot, humid, and coastal climates.

directions: Plant individual cloves pointed end up about two inches deep in the fall. This California-bred silverskin softneck yields ten to twelve cloves per bulb and is prized for consistent performance and high yields. It adapts well across cold, hot, humid, and coastal climates and offers excellent long-term storage. Mulch after planting, then keep the bed evenly moist through the spring growing period. Harvest when lower leaves brown and cure the bulbs in a dry, airy place for months of storage.

planting.note: Start indoors in early to midsummer for a fall harvest sweetened by frost.

directions: Start seed indoors in early to midsummer and transplant sturdy seedlings for a long-season fall crop. Brussels sprouts need a lengthy, cool finish, and a light frost actually sweetens the tight little sprouts. Space plants generously and stake tall stalks in windy sites to keep them upright. Remove yellowing lower leaves as sprouts form to improve air flow and size. Harvest from the bottom of the stalk upward once sprouts are firm and about an inch across.

planting.note: Direct sow in spring and fall; more heat tolerant than standard arugula.

directions: Direct sow seed shallowly in cool weather and keep the bed moist for quick, even germination. As the name suggests, this variety resists bolting longer than standard arugula and tolerates a bit more heat. Harvest young leaves for microgreens or baby greens, or let plants reach full size for peppery salad leaves. Cut outer leaves and let the center regrow for repeated harvests. Successive sowings every couple of weeks keep a steady supply of tender greens.

planting.note: Direct sow; bred to stay leafy longer before flowering.

directions: Direct sow seed where plants are to grow, as cilantro has a taproot and dislikes transplanting. This beginner-friendly variety is bred to stay leafy longer before bolting to seed, extending the harvest of flat, flavorful leaves. Plants reach about twelve to twenty-four inches and respond well to successive sowings every few weeks. Harvest outer leaves regularly to encourage fresh growth and delay flowering. If plants do bolt, let them go to seed to collect coriander.

planting.note: Start indoors; good for short-season areas thanks to its early maturity.

directions: Start seed indoors and transplant sturdy seedlings into rich, evenly moist soil for a spring or fall crop. This dependable variety forms flavorful six-inch snow-white heads in about seventy to eighty days, making it well suited to short-season gardens. The leaves naturally curl around the head for self-blanching and protection from sun scald. Keep water and nutrients steady, since stress can trigger loose or off-color curds. Mature heads hold well in the garden, so they need not all be cut at once.

common_name: Soil Builder Peas and Oats Cover Crop

planting.note: Broadcast in spring or late summer to build and protect soil.

directions: Broadcast this field pea and oat mix densely over prepared soil in spring or late summer, then rake in and water. The fast-growing combination adds organic matter and nutrients while shading out weeds. Pea plants fix nitrogen and the oats provide structure and biomass, together conditioning even rough ground such as former lawns and vacant lots. Let the stand grow until flowering, then cut and turn it under a few weeks before planting your main crop. The oats often winterkill in cold regions, leaving an easy-to-manage mulch.

planting.note: Direct sow in spring; can also be started indoors for transplants.

directions: Direct sow seed thickly in shallow rows in early spring and keep the bed evenly moist. This specialty bunching onion forms uniform, sweet, tender stalks with only a small bulb at the base, prized for grilled cebollitas asadas. Vigorous and easy to grow, it can also be started indoors and transplanted in clumps. Hill a little soil around the stalks to lengthen and blanch the white bases. Harvest whenever stalks reach usable size, pulling entire plants as needed.

planting.note: Start indoors and transplant after frost danger passes.

directions: Start seed indoors six to eight weeks before the last frost and transplant into warm soil once frost danger passes. This F1 hybrid produces extra-large, dark green, thick-walled four-inch jalapenos with a slightly above-average heat around 6,500 Scoville units. The disease-resistant plants are high yielding and the fruit has an exceptional shelf life. Pick peppers green and firm for classic use, or let some ripen red for added sweetness. Steady water and full sun keep the heavy sets coming all season.

planting.note: Direct sow in cool weather; good bolt tolerance for spring and fall.

directions: Direct sow seed in cool soil in early spring or late summer and keep the bed consistently moist. This upright, compact spinach produces smooth to lightly savoyed, medium-green leaves that are excellent fresh or sauteed. High resistance to downy mildew and good bolting tolerance make it dependable across seasons. Harvest outer leaves as needed or cut whole plants at the base for a full crop. Successive sowings extend the harvest before summer heat triggers bolting.

planting.note: Direct sow after frost in warm soil; compact vines suit containers.

directions: Direct sow seed after the last frost once the soil is thoroughly warm, or start indoors in peat pots for an early start. This compact variety carries full-sized slicing cucumbers on short two-to-three-foot vines, making it ideal for small beds and containers. Keep plants evenly watered, especially during flowering and fruiting, to avoid bitter or misshapen cucumbers. Harvest fruits young and often to keep the vines productive. A little support keeps fruit clean and straight even in tight spaces.

planting.note: Start indoors early; transplant crowns to a permanent bed after frost.

directions: Start seed indoors early, as germination is slow and warmth-dependent, then transplant to a permanent, weed-free bed after frost. This vigorous, all-male hybrid produces eight-to-ten-inch spears and, being male-sterile, channels its energy into strong crown and root growth rather than berries. Patience pays off, since harvest begins in the third year but a healthy bed can produce for up to twelve years. Feed and mulch the bed annually and let ferns grow after harvest to build the crowns. Harvest spears for only a few weeks the first productive year, then longer as plants mature.

planting.note: Direct sow after frost; needs a long, warm season and a sturdy trellis.

directions: Direct sow seed after the last frost in warm soil, or start indoors to gain time in short seasons. These vigorous vines need a long, warm season and benefit from a strong trellis that lets the swan-necked fruits hang and shape naturally. The emerald green shells speckled with light green make striking autumn displays. For crafts, let gourds mature fully on the vine, then cure them in a dry, airy place for weeks to months until the shells harden. Cured hard-shelled gourds last indefinitely and take paint and carving well.

planting.note: Sow densely in cool weather; can also be grown indoors year-round.

directions: Sow seed densely in shallow rows or broad bands and keep the surface moist for even germination. Fast-growing baby spinach leaves add flavor and nutrients to salads, sandwiches, smoothies, and pesto. This crop can be grown indoors at any time of year, so move containers as needed for the best light and cool temperatures. Harvest leaves young, when just a few inches tall, by cutting above the growing point so plants regrow. Successive sowings keep tender baby leaves coming through the cool seasons.

planting.note: Direct sow after frost in warm soil; needs room to vine.

directions: Direct sow seed after the last frost in warm, rich soil, planting in hills with plenty of room for the vines to run. This small pie pumpkin, a 1966 heirloom crossing Sugar Pie and Jack O'Lantern, averages six to seven inches tall with sweet, fine-textured yellow-orange flesh. Give plants steady water and consistent feeding for good fruit set and size. Harvest when the rind hardens and turns fully colored, leaving a length of stem attached. Cure the fruit in a warm spot to improve flavor and storage life.

planting.note: Direct sow after frost in blocks for good pollination.

directions: Direct sow seed after the last frost in warm soil, planting in blocks of several short rows to ensure good pollination. Each compact four-foot stalk produces two or more ruby-red, strawberry-shaped ears two to three inches long. The ornamental foliage and ears look striking at the back of a border as well as in the vegetable patch. Let the ears dry fully on the stalk, then harvest and cure the kernels before popping. Keep this popcorn separated from other corn types to avoid cross-pollination.

planting.note: Direct sow as soon as soil can be worked in early spring.

directions: Direct sow seed as soon as the soil can be worked in early spring, since peas thrive in cool weather. Bred by the developer of the original snap pea, this improved variety offers stringless three-inch pods, shorter vines, and better disease resistance. The compact plants need only light support and produce sweet, crunchy edible pods. Keep the soil evenly moist during flowering and pod fill for the best flavor and yield. Pick pods often while young and plump to keep the plants producing.

planting.note: Direct sow in early spring; tall vines need a sturdy trellis.

directions: Direct sow seed in early spring as soon as the soil can be worked and set up a tall, sturdy trellis. This striking variety bears tender, fine-flavored purple edible pods and lovely purple flowers on six-to-seven-foot vines. Long hypertendrils act as extra supports, helping the vigorous plants cling to their trellis. Keep soil evenly moist during flowering and pod set for the best flavor. Harvest pods young and often while the vines climb, picking before they toughen.

planting.note: Direct sow after frost in warm soil; give vines room to run.

directions: Direct sow seed after the last frost in warm, rich soil, planting in hills with ample space for the vines. This heirloom, a standard since the 1800s, produces small six-to-seven-inch fruits with sweet flesh ideal for pies, soups, muffins, and casseroles. The compact fruits also work for small carving jack-o'-lanterns. Provide steady water and feeding for good fruit set, and harvest when the rind is hard and fully colored. Cured fruit stores for months in a cool, dry place.

planting.note: Direct sow in early spring; tall vines benefit from a trellis.

directions: Direct sow seed as soon as the soil can be worked in early spring and provide a trellis for the tall vines. This All-America Selections winner produces an extended harvest of thick, crunchy three-inch edible pods bursting with sweet flavor. The vigorous plants have some heat tolerance but still perform best in cool weather. Keep soil evenly moist during flowering and pod fill for tender, sweet pods. Pick regularly while young to encourage continued production and enjoy them fresh off the vine.

planting.note: Start indoors and transplant after frost danger passes.

directions: Start seed indoors about six weeks before the last frost and transplant seedlings out after frost danger passes. This indeterminate favorite produces long clusters of plump, tangerine-colored cherry tomatoes that are exceptionally sweet and juicy. Provide a tall stake or cage, since the vigorous vines easily reach six feet or more. Keep watering even to reduce cracking and pick fruit at peak color, or a bit early to finish ripening indoors. These low-acid tomatoes are rarely found in stores and are a special garden treat.

planting.note: Grow indoors in trays year-round; not dependent on outdoor season.

directions: Soak the black oil sunflower seed for several hours, then sow densely across a shallow tray of moist growing medium. Keep the tray covered and dark until the seedlings push up, then move to bright light to green up. These nutrient-dense greens are high in iron, calcium, protein, and healthy fats and can be grown indoors year-round. Harvest by snipping the stems just above the soil once the first true leaves begin to show, usually within about ten days. Keep the medium evenly moist and provide good airflow to prevent mold.

planting.note: Start indoors and transplant after frost danger passes.

directions: Start seed indoors about six weeks before the last frost and transplant seedlings out once frost danger passes. This determinate Roma sets a dependable mid-season crop of large, uniform paste tomatoes even in heat. The concentrated harvest is ideal for making sauce or paste, and the meaty fruits also chop well for salads and salsas. As a bush type it needs only light support and produces most of its fruit over a short window. In long-season areas a successive planting extends the harvest.

planting.note: Start indoors and transplant after frost danger passes.

directions: Start seed indoors six to eight weeks before the last frost and transplant into warm soil after frost danger passes. This 1941 Hungarian heirloom is the traditional pickling type, bearing long, bright yellow, thick-walled sweet peppers. Enjoy the fruits fresh as a snack, in salads, grilled, or in any recipe calling for sweet pepper. Left on the plant, the peppers ripen through orange to red and grow even sweeter. Keep plants evenly watered and pick regularly to encourage a heavy set.

planting.note: Start indoors or direct sow after frost in warm soil.

directions: Start seed indoors in peat pots a few weeks before the last frost, or direct sow once the soil is warm, giving vines plenty of room. This honeydew ripens to an unbelievably sweet, almost wine-like flavor far superior to store-bought melons. Each seven-by-eight-inch fruit weighs about seven to eight pounds with light green flesh and skin. Water consistently while vines grow and fruit sets, then ease off as melons ripen to concentrate sugars. Harvest when the blossom end softens and the rind lightens to creamy color.

planting.note: Start indoors and transplant after frost danger passes.

directions: Start seed indoors about six weeks before the last frost and set plants out once frost danger passes. This indeterminate heirloom-style cherry tomato is known for hearty, sweet flavor and an early harvest. It produces large numbers of three-quarter to one-inch fruits in grape-like clusters on vigorous vines that easily reach six feet. Provide sturdy support and keep watering even to reduce cracking of these low-acid tomatoes. Pick clusters as they ripen to keep new fruit setting all season.

planting.note: Direct sow after frost; compact vines suit small gardens.

directions: Direct sow seed after the last frost in warm soil, or start indoors in peat pots for an early crop. This bush-type acorn squash matures early on compact four-foot vines, making it great for small gardens, large containers, and short seasons. The fruits offer the rich, sweet flavor of baked winter squash prized on cool autumn days. Water steadily during vine growth and fruit set, then let the rinds harden fully before harvest. Cure and store the squash in a cool, dry place for use through fall and winter.

planting.note: Direct sow after frost in warm soil; grow on a trellis for straight fruit.

directions: Direct sow seed after the last frost in warm soil, or start indoors in peat pots to gain a head start. This Japanese-type cucumber bears slender, long, burpless fruits with thin, sweet, non-bitter skin and very few tiny seeds. Grow the highly productive plants on a trellis to save space and encourage straight, clean fruit. Keep watering even to prevent bitterness and misshapen cucumbers. Harvest frequently while fruits are still young and tender to keep the vines producing.

planting.note: Direct sow after frost in warm soil; no staking needed.

directions: Direct sow seed after the last frost once the soil is warm, planting in rows or blocks for easy picking. This French filet bean, or haricot vert, is at its best when harvested young and slender and picked frequently. The disease-resistant bush plants need no staking and grow well in containers. Keep the soil evenly moist during flowering and pod set for tender, straight pods. Pick every couple of days at petite size to keep the plants producing over a long window.

planting.note: Direct sow after frost in warm soil; grow on a trellis for long, straight fruit.

directions: Direct sow seed after the last frost in warm soil, or start indoors in peat pots for an early crop. This English-type heirloom from the 1800s bears long, thin-skinned, burpless fruits up to eighteen inches with very small, soft seeds. Originally bred for greenhouses, it now thrives outdoors and produces best when trained up a trellis. Keep watering even to keep fruit straight and free of bitterness. Harvest the long cucumbers while young and tender for the sweetest flavor.

planting.note: Direct sow after frost; tolerant of cool soil conditions.

directions: Direct sow seed after the last frost, though this variety tolerates cooler soil better than most cucumbers. The thin-skinned, sweet, burpless, acid-free fruits are a delight harvested at eight inches or shorter. Pick early for pickling or let fruit grow to full size as a slicer, since it works well for both. Tendergreen resists powdery mildew, adding to its reliability in the garden. Keep watering even and harvest frequently to keep the vigorous vines productive.

planting.note: Direct sow spring through fall; tolerates both heat and cold well.

directions: Direct sow seed shallowly and keep the bed moist for quick germination. This versatile Japanese green, also called komatsuna, is milder than most mustards and very easy to grow. It quickly produces flat, smooth, dark, glossy green leaves and tolerates both heat and cold better than spinach. Harvest outer leaves as needed or cut whole plants young for tender greens. Successive sowings from spring through fall keep a steady supply for stir-fries and salads.
